serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw lean ham is approximately 60 g (about 2 thin slices), providing roughly 88 kcal, 13.4 g protein, and 910 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Ham, raw, lean — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Ham, raw, lean (F06110014). All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat not reported in the HPB entry; these are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10178 — Ham, raw, lean) as the closest approved-source equivalent and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GI not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ham, raw, lean contains only 0.05 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign a GI value to foods with negligible carbohydrate content. Per the Veritas scoring model for low/zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to 0 as the numeric sentinel for not-applicable (gi_category = not_applicable), and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1516 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit raw lean ham; even a 60 g serving delivers approximately 910 mg sodium, far exceeding the recommended 600 mg per meal threshold, and the high sodium load directly raises blood pressure through fluid retention and vascular resistance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.05 g/100 g) but high sodium and processed curing agents
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw lean ham has negligible carbohydrate and will not raise blood glucose directly; however, its very high sodium content (1516 mg/100 g) may worsen insulin resistance over time, and processed curing agents including sodium nitrates have been associated with adverse metabolic effects, so portion size should be limited and fresh lean pork preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1516 mg/100 g), saturated fat (1.93 g/100 g), cholesterol (52 mg/100 g), and processed curing agents
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of raw lean ham due to its very high sodium content driving hypertensive cardiovascular risk, combined with saturated fat and cholesterol contributing to atherogenic lipid profiles, and epidemiological evidence linking processed red meat consumption to increased cardiovascular mortality.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory processed cured meat with high sodium and sodium nitrates
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ham is a processed cured pork product containing sodium nitrates and high sodium (1516 mg/100 g), both of which are pro-inflammatory and may worsen rheumatoid arthritis-related joint inflammation; substitution with fresh poultry or fatty fish is strongly recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ham, raw, lean is a salt-cured pork product derived from the hind leg of the pig, with all separable internal and external fat removed to yield a lean muscle-only cut. The deep pink to reddish colour is characteristic of the salt-curing and nitrate-based preservation process. In its raw state it has a firm, dense texture and a mildly saline aroma from the curing salts. Per 100 g it provides 147 kcal, 22.32 g protein, 5.71 g fat (of which approximately 1.93 g is saturated), and only 0.05 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate food. However, its sodium content is extremely high at 1516 mg per 100 g — more than double the 600 mg threshold considered safe for hypertensive individuals — due to the salt-curing process. Cholesterol is 52 mg per 100 g and potassium is 371 mg per 100 g. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium load, and should be used with caution in type 2 diabetes management given the metabolic effects of processed curing agents. It is not suitable for those with rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is not applicable as carbohydrate content is negligible. Ham is widely used in Western cuisines as a breakfast protein, sandwich filling, or ingredient in cooked dishes, but the raw lean form is typically an intermediate preparation state before cooking.
common_user_queries: Is ham good for weight loss?
How much sodium is in raw ham?
Can I eat ham if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between raw ham and cooked ham?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database entry F06110014 at confidence 0.95. Fatty acid breakdown (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat) cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 10178 (Ham, raw, lean) as the closest approved-source equivalent, documented in notes. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a lean cured pork product.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is 0.05 g per 100 g — this is a near-zero but non-zero value confirmed by HPB source; it is not naturally zero but is negligible. Sugar_g is 0.0 g per 100 g: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F06110014. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g per 100 g: naturally zero as this is a pure muscle meat product with no plant fibre. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.05 g/100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to 0 as the numeric sentinel value per template requirements. Saturated_fat_g (1.93 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.68 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.62 g), and trans_fat_g (0.04 g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 10178 (Ham, raw, lean) as the HPB source does not report fatty acid breakdown; this adaptation is documented under nutrient_source. Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB or USDA SR Legacy for this specific entry): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed vitamin and mineral panel not reported in HPB Foodo F06110014 and not available in USDA SR Legacy NDB 10178 at the required specificity for lean raw ham. Halal status is not confirmed as ham is derived from pork; is_halal set to false. This food is not suitable for Muslim dietary requirements. The input record referenced rheumatoid_arthritis suitability with a claim of trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g; USDA SR Legacy cross-reference yields trans_fat_g of approximately 0.04 g/100 g, which is the value used here. The RA health context is retained as an additional condition given the pro-inflammatory profile of processed cured meats.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (1516.0), saturated_fat_g (1.93 — adapted from USDA SR Legacy NDB 10178, documented in notes), and sugar_g (0.0) are all present as numeric values and plausible for lean cured pork.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to 0 as the numeric sentinel value required by the template for not-applicable foods; gi_category set to 'not_applicable'; glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0; gi_source populated with full rationale explaining that carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.05 g/100 g and GI is not applicable. The validator feedback from iteration 2 indicated glycemic_index must be numeric — this is now satisfied with the value 0 as the numeric sentinel, paired with gi_category 'not_applicable' to signal the not-applicable state.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Ham, Raw, Lean (F06110014) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The q4 issue across iterations was resolved by setting glycemic_index to 0 as a numeric sentinel (satisfying the template requirement that the field be numeric) while gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' to correctly signal that GI is not meaningful for this negligible-carbohydrate food. Core macros are sourced from HPB Foodo at high confidence. Fatty acid breakdown is adapted from USDA SR Legacy NDB 10178 and documented. All three mandatory health conditions are present with clinical specificity. Ontology anchors are defensible and not invented. Aliases are consistent between flat array and structured object. No mandatory non-English language names are required for this Western generic food.
